sources.notes: Used to confirm the high-to-low bit construction, tight/loose state split, and optimality by bit weight dominance.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: For each test case, construct k integers a_i with 0 <= a_i <= n, XOR equal to n, and maximum possible total sum.
annotation.constraints: n fits in about 30 bits and k can be output explicitly, so an O(k log n) constructive algorithm is intended.
annotation.core_idea: Process bits from high to low. XOR fixes only the parity of how many a_i receive a 1 at each bit, while the objective wants as many 1s as possible because one higher bit dominates all lower bits. Maintain which indices are still tight with n and which are already loose; tight indices cannot take a 1 when n has 0, while loose indices can. When n has a 1 and k is even, one zero is forced, and choosing it from a tight index creates a new loose number for later zero-bits.
annotation.complexity: O(k log n) per test case plus output size.

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Build all a_i bit by bit. For a 1-bit of n, set an odd and maximal number of ones; for a 0-bit, set an even and maximal number of ones only among loose indices.

tags.evidence: The construction greedily decides every bit from high to low, maximizing the number of chosen 1-bits subject to XOR parity and the a_i <= n bound.
tags.description: Greedy decisions over binary bits, usually from high to low, where higher-bit value dominates lower-bit choices and feasibility is checked with bit constraints.
tags.created_reason: The existing bitmask tag is too broad for problems whose main retrieval feature is a high-to-low bit greedy proof.

tags.evidence: The algorithm distinguishes indices whose current prefix equals n from indices already below n; this state determines whether later zero-bits may be set.
tags.description: Maintain objects as tight to an upper bound or already loose below it, so future choices are constrained differently.
tags.created_reason: This tight/loose boundary is common in bit/digit constructions but is not captured by generic constructive or bitmask tags.

tags.evidence: The output is the actual optimal array, and the proof shows the bit decisions construct a feasible optimum.
At each bit the algorithm keeps as many 1s as possible because any higher-bit gain dominates all lower-bit changes.
The feasibility and objective are both reasoned bitwise through XOR parity.
